text: A database is an organised collection of data. Most databases today are either relational (SQL) or non-relational (NoSQL). In SQL databases, data is stored in tables with rows and columns, like a spreadsheet. You use queries, written in the SQL language, to ask questions or find specific information. Before building a database, you need data modelling – this means planning what tables to create and how they relate to each other. To make searches faster, databases use indexes. An index is like a book's contents page: it tells the database where to find data quickly without checking every row. NoSQL databases, on the other hand, store data in flexible formats like documents, which can be better for certain modern applications.
